data.text/markdown: Build an interactive customer segmentation dashboard in a marimo notebook (hackathon.py) using the UCI Online Retail dataset — that tells a business "who your customers are and what to do about each group."

Goal 1 — Get the data in

Load the Online Retail dataset, then clean it so downstream cells only see trustworthy transactions:
  • Drop rows with missing CustomerID
  • Remove returns using non-positive Quantity (this UCI export does not include InvoiceNo, so we cannot match credit-note prefixes; quantity sign is the reliable return signal here)
  • Filter bad rows: drop non-positive UnitPrice, blank Description, and rows where InvoiceDate cannot be parsed
Output: one retail_clean dataframe, ready for customer-level aggregation and segmentation.

data.text/markdown:
Goal 2 — Visual storytelling + RFM scoring

Charts set the scene: revenue over time, top countries, top products (by line description), and an overview card with headline KPIs. RFM per customer (this export has no invoice numbers):
  • Recency — days since last purchase (relative to the latest timestamp in the cleaned data)
  • Frequency — number of distinct purchase calendar days per customer (proxy for repeat buying / order occasions)
  • Monetary — total spend, sum of Quantity * UnitPrice
Each of R, F, and M is scored 1–5 using quintiles (more recent purchases earn a higher R; higher activity and spend earn higher F and M).

data.text/markdown:
Goal 3 — Segment customers

Assign every customer to a named lifecycle segment from their RFM scores (priority-ordered rules so labels stay mutually exclusive). Then review segment sizes in a colorful bar chart to see where the base concentrates (Champions, Loyal, At Risk, Lost, New, and a few supporting buckets).

Goal 3 · Segment distribution

data.text/markdown:
Goal 4 — Make it interactive

Pick a segment from the dropdown (next cell). Two Altair charts summarize every segment at once: customer counts and total revenue (sum of per-customer monetary, GBP). The table at the bottom reacts to your choice and lists customers in that segment (sorted by spend).

Goal 4 · Customers in the selected segment

data.text/markdown:
Goal 5 — AI layer (offline)

Insights are pre-generated and stored as ai_insights.json next to this notebook — no API keys and no live model calls at runtime. The cell below loads the JSON and renders styled cards (HTML in markdown) with emoji, bold headings, accent borders, and soft backgrounds per segment, plus expandable Q&A snippets.

Pre-generated segment playbooks

Plain text only. No API keys and no live model calls in the notebook. Edit this JSON to refresh copy, then re-run the Goal 5 cell.

🏆 Champions

Who they are — Your best customers: very recent purchases, strong repeat activity, and high spend. They behave like fans of the assortment and keep the cash register moving.

Recommended play — Champions → double down on loyalty rewards, early access drops, and referral perks. Ask for reviews and UGC; protect margin with bundles instead of blanket discounts.

💙 Loyal Customers

Who they are — High frequency and solid monetary value with decent recency. They are dependable repeat buyers even if they are not quite in the top RFM corner.

Recommended play — Loyal Customers → nurture with subscription-style reminders, replenishment prompts for consumables, and tiered perks that reward consistency over one-off coupons.

🌱 Promising

Who they are — Recent engagement with meaningful spend but frequency still has room to grow. They look like customers on an upswing if you give them reasons to return.

Recommended play — Promising → cross-sell complementary SKUs, limited-time bundles, and educational content (how-to, styling, care guides) that justify a second purchase soon.

✨ New

Who they are — Very recent first or early purchases with low frequency so far. They are still forming habits and have not proven long-term loyalty yet.

Recommended play — New → onboarding series, satisfaction check-ins, and a clear second-purchase incentive tied to categories they already bought. Keep service fast to cement trust.

🧭 Needs Attention

Who they are — Middle-of-the-road RFM scores: not cold, not hot. They are easy to ignore but often respond to a nudge with the right offer.

Recommended play — Needs Attention → light personalization (category affinity), small experiments on subject lines and send time, and surveys to learn blockers like shipping or assortment gaps.

⚠️ At Risk

Who they are — Historically engaged (frequency and spend were healthy) but recency has slipped. They look like drift, not beginners.

Recommended play — At Risk → win-back campaign with a sincere "we miss you" tone, replenishment reminders, winback codes with a deadline, and optional human outreach for high monetary tiers.

💎 Cannot Lose Them

Who they are — Big spenders whose recency has gone stale. Losing them would hurt revenue disproportionately even if counts are small.

Recommended play — Cannot Lose Them → white-glove save offers, proactive service recovery, VIP concierge, and inventory alerts on favorites. Avoid spam; prioritize relevance and respect.

😴 Hibernating

Who they are — Quiet lately with modest past engagement signals. They are not the worst churn profile but they are fading from active memory of the brand.

Recommended play — Hibernating → low-frequency reactivation cadence, seasonal lookbooks, and "what is new since you left" highlights. Sunset to cheaper channels if no response after a few tries.

🪦 Lost

Who they are — Weak recency, frequency, and monetary pattern. They behave like one-time or long-lapsed shoppers with little recent signal to build on.

Recommended play — Lost → suppress expensive paid reactivation, run rare high-value tests only, and focus acquisition learnings on why they never graduated to repeat buyers.

Quick Q&A

Q: Why is there no live AI call in this notebook?
A: The hackathon layer is intentionally offline: insights are authored once as JSON so the dashboard stays fast, reproducible, and free of API keys in the UI.
Q: How should I refresh the copy?
A: Edit ai_insights.json (or regenerate it elsewhere), save the file beside hackathon-notebook.py, then re-run the Goal 5 insight cell in marimo.
Q: How does "revenue per segment" relate to RFM monetary?
A: Monetary here is the per-customer sum of line revenue in the cleaned data; segment revenue is that customer-level monetary summed for everyone in the segment.

Goal 6 — Polish & demo

Executive snapshot at the end of the story: hero KPIs, a segment recap strip (emoji + colors aligned with ai_insights.json and your charts), a highlighted takeaway, and a thank-you card for the hackathon.
